perturbation.original_text: 1.4) "Evaluation" means an evaluation by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's Samples for the purpose of testing against those methods as specified in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. All tests available reside in the Petro-Lube Testing Brochure or on the Petro-Lube web site or by quotation.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4) "Evaluation" means an evaluation by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's Samples for the purpose of testing against those methods the Receiving Party deems appropriate, regardless of specifications in the Disclosing Party's Purchase Order. The Receiving Party is not bound by any specifications, brochures, web sites, or quotations from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text redefines "Evaluation" to give the Receiving Party (Petro-Lube) complete discretion in testing methods, contradicting the original clause that ties it to the Disclosing Party's (Your Company Name) purchase order and specified methods. This creates uncertainty and shifts control to Petro-Lube, potentially invalidating the original intent of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Fitness for Particular Purpose
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the reliance on the Disclosing Party's specifications, the Receiving Party is not held to the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose. If the Disclosing Party specified the use of test, there is an implied warranty that the testing methods are suitable. By modifying 'Evaluation' to remove this requirement, this conflicts with UCC § 2-315.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-315. Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-315. Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose. Where the seller at the time of contracting has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is unless excluded or modified under the next section an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-315, which establishes an implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose when the seller knows the buyer's specific needs and the buyer relies on the seller's expertise. By allowing the Receiving Party (buyer) to use any testing methods they deem appropriate, regardless of the Disclosing Party's (seller) specifications, the modified text removes the Disclosing Party's ability to ensure the goods are tested in a manner fit for their intended purpose. This eliminates the implied warranty that would exist if the Receiving Party followed the Disclosing Party's specified testing methods. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "where the seller at the time of contracting has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is unless excluded or modified under the next section an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose." By giving the buyer full discretion in testing methods, the agreement effectively excludes the implied warranty, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2) Nothing in this Agreement shall operate to exclude or limit the liability of either Party for death or personal injury arising from its negligence, fraudulent misrepresentation or any other liability to the extent the same may not be excluded or limited as a matter of law.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2) The Parties agree that any claims for death or personal injury arising from negligence or fraudulent misrepresentation shall be subject to a maximum liability of $10,000, regardless of the extent to which such liability may not be excluded or limited as a matter of law.
perturbation.explanation: This modification caps the liability for death or personal injury, directly contradicting the original clause which stated that such liability could not be excluded or limited to the extent prohibited by law. This creates a legal contradiction because it attempts to limit liability in situations where the law may not allow it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: State laws regarding wrongful death and personal injury liability.
perturbation.law_citation: NJ Rev Stat § 2A:31-1 (Wrongful Death Act - Example for New Jersey)
perturbation.law_explanation: Wrongful death statutes, such as the New Jersey statute cited above, typically allow for recovery of damages commensurate with the loss suffered by the deceased's family. The modified text attempts to cap this recovery at $10,000, which directly contradicts the purpose and intent of wrongful death laws that aim to provide full compensation for losses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2A:31-1 - When action lies :: 2013 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New Jersey Revised Statutes 2024 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2023 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2022 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2013 N.J. Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2013 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 2A - ADMINISTRATION OF CIVIL AND CRIMINAL JUSTICE Section 2A:31-1 - When action lies NJ Rev Stat § 2A:31-1 (2013) What's This? 2A:31-1. When action lies When the death of a person is caused by a wrongful act, neglect or default, such as would, if death had not ensued, have entitled the person injured to maintain an action for damages resulting from the injury, the person who would have been liable in damages for the injury if death had not ensued shall be liable in an action for damages, notwithstanding the death of the person injured and although the death was caused under circumstances amounting in law to a crime. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a maximum liability of $10,000 for death or personal injury arising from negligence or fraudulent misrepresentation, which directly contradicts the original clause that stated such liability could not be excluded or limited. The New Jersey statute 2A:31-1 states that when death is caused by a wrongful act, the person liable for the injury shall be liable for damages, 'notwithstanding the death of the person injured'. By capping the liability at $10,000, the changed text limits the damages recoverable, undermining the statute's intent to provide compensation for the losses resulting from the wrongful act, regardless of the injured person's death. The altered contract directly opposes the original clause's intention to exclude any limitations on liability where prohibited by law. The modification removes a core legal obligation of providing compensation commensurate with the loss suffered.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1) Petro-Lube certifies that, in exercising its rights and carrying out its obligations under this Agreement, it shall comply with all applicable governmental laws, regulations, decrees and orders governing the export and re-export of goods, technology and software including, without limitation, the U.S. Export Administration Regulations and European Council Regulation 428/2009 and all amendments to such Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1) Petro-Lube certifies that, in exercising its rights and carrying out its obligations under this Agreement, it shall comply with most applicable governmental laws, regulations, decrees and orders governing the export and re-export of goods, technology and software, except when such compliance would significantly impede Petro-Lube's ability to fulfill its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a significant exception to export control compliance. By adding 'except when such compliance would significantly impede Petro-Lube's ability to fulfill its obligations,' the agreement allows Petro-Lube to selectively disregard export regulations. This creates ambiguity and directly contradicts the original intent of strict compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict adherence to export control regulations. Allowing Petro-Lube to disregard these regulations when they 'significantly impede' its ability to fulfill its obligations directly violates the mandatory compliance required by the EAR, rendering the clause legally questionable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception allowing Petro-Lube to disregard export regulations if compliance 'would significantly impede Petro-Lube's ability to fulfill its obligations.' This contradicts the requirement for strict adherence to export control regulations. The Law Explanation states that 'allowing Petro-Lube to disregard these regulations when they 'significantly impede' its ability to fulfill its obligations directly violates the mandatory compliance required by the EAR.' Although the snippets themselves don't explicitly state mandatory compliance, the Law Explanation referencing the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) in conjunction with the Perturbation Explanation describing the removal of strict compliance establishes a direct contradiction. The modification creates an exception where no exception is permitted under applicable export control regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4) At the conclusion of the Evaluation Petro-Lube shall either return promptly to (Your Company Name) or it's Assigns, or at (Your Company Name)'s option, destroy any remaining Sample in whatever form then in its possession or control and verify such to (Your Company Name).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4) At the conclusion of the Evaluation, Petro-Lube shall retain all remaining Samples for internal research purposes, unless (Your Company Name) specifically requests their return or destruction within one year of the Evaluation's completion. If no request is made, Petro-Lube may dispose of the samples as it sees fit.
perturbation.explanation: This modification changes the default action regarding the Samples after evaluation. Originally, Petro-Lube was obligated to return or destroy the samples. The modified text allows Petro-Lube to retain the samples for internal research unless explicitly instructed otherwise, potentially conflicting with intellectual property rights if (Your Company Name) considers the samples proprietary.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the samples contain or embody (Your Company Name)'s trade secrets, retaining and using them for internal research without explicit permission could violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act. The original agreement ensured return or destruction, mitigating this risk. The modified text increases the risk of misappropriation by allowing Petro-Lube to retain the samples indefinitely unless specifically requested otherwise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Defend Trade Secrets Act by allowing Petro-Lube to retain samples that may contain (Your Company Name)'s trade secrets for internal research without explicit permission. This is a direct contradiction of the trade secret protection provided by the Act. The original agreement ensured return or destruction, mitigating this risk. The modified text increases the risk of misappropriation by allowing Petro-Lube to retain the samples indefinitely unless specifically requested otherwise. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1836, outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, indicating that an owner of a trade secret can bring a civil action if the trade secret is misappropriated. By allowing Petro-Lube to retain the samples, the modified contract creates a situation where misappropriation is more likely to occur, thus contradicting the legal protections afforded to trade secrets.
